dossiers.bear_case: Classic leveraged-spin structure: $16B of new debt raised against a business that carried $0 debt as a Honeywell segment, funding (via a $1.125B trademark-license payment schedule to Honeywell over <5 years) what amounts to a value transfer back toward the parent. Pro forma equity flips to a $(3,454)M deficit. Tax indemnification to Honeywell is explicitly uncapped ('not expected to be limited in amount or subject to any cap'). Environmental liabilities of $823-827M are broadly assigned to spinco including legacy sites. No insider has put a dollar of open-market cash behind the stock yet -- management's stake is 100% passive conversion of pre-existing awards, all named officers under 1% individually. This is the textbook 'spinco keeps the debt' pattern Greenblatt warns to pass on, not a garbage-barge-free orphan.
dossiers.bull_case: A genuinely large ($17.4B revenue), well-known aerospace/defense supplier (avionics, environmental control systems, APU/propulsion via E&PS, Federal Solutions managing DOE sites) spun out of Honeywell with standard equity-conversion incentive alignment and a real net pension ASSET contribution ($2.727B) rather than the usual dumped pension liability. If it holds S&P-500-scale index inclusion, the initial mechanical share flood clears within weeks and a business this large and well-covered re-rates on fundamentals, not on being discovered -- the opposite of the small-orphan thesis, but low variance.
dossiers.forced_seller_map: Distribution ratio 1-for-2 (Honeywell:Aerospace), ~316.9M HONA shares distributed, listed on Nasdaq (not a venue-mismatch trigger since parent Honeywell also trades Nasdaq: HON). FY2025 net sales of $17.4B and pro forma net income of ~$2.36B are unambiguously S&P-500 scale, arguing against a too-small-to-keep ejection, but the Form 10 discloses no index-committee decision either way (outside company control, not addressed in the filing). Risk Factors carry only generic forced-selling boilerplate: 'unable to predict whether large amounts of our common stock will be sold in the open market following the distribution.' No dividend has been declared yet (only a forward-looking intent to pay one), so yield-mandate-driven selling is unconfirmed. Net: a real dump is plausible given the mechanical share flood to every Honeywell holder, but the filing gives no specific index-exit catalyst to size it.
dossiers.key_risk: Uncapped tax indemnification to Honeywell combined with $16B of new debt and negative pro forma equity -- any adverse tax determination or refinancing stress lands on a balance sheet with no equity cushion.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: Form 4 sweep since 2026-06-26 distribution: no open-market purchases, no sales. The single transaction on file is Honeywell International's own Form 4 (10% owner, code J, 316,939,750 shares at $0 -- the distribution mechanics itself), not a market signal. Only ~1 week post-distribution; thin by construction, re-sweep on later runs.
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: Pro forma adjustments are itemized (notes a-q) but several soften the headline picture: $211M transaction costs + $68M standalone dis-synergies are flagged by the company itself as uncertain estimates; Adjusted EBIT / segment profit exclude a $373M Flexjet litigation settlement that hit historical GAAP net income; pro forma EPS ($5.62 FY2025) rests on a share count that is not yet finalized. Most importantly, the capitalization table footnote admits the pro forma is incomplete: 'Aerospace has not yet finalized its post-distribution capitalization ... will be included in an amendment.' The negative pro forma equity of $(3,454)M is disclosed plainly, not hidden, but the filing does not editorialize on what negative equity means for credit ratings or covenants.

dossiers.bear_case: Debt-funded pre-spin dividend to parent ($233M actual, vs $229M modeled, drawn from a freshly entered $1.0B secured credit facility) is the standard leveraged-spin pattern, though modest in absolute size relative to peers in this batch (HONA's $16B, MBGL's $2B). Company explicitly discloses its own pro forma omits real standalone dis-synergy and TSA costs, and the debt/dividend terms were still 'preliminary and subject to change' as filed -- already proven unreliable once actuals landed. Uncapped-style bidirectional indemnification with a two-year tax-driven restrictive covenant (prohibiting >40% ownership-change transactions) narrows strategic flexibility just as the company needs to prove itself standalone. No insider Form 4 data exists yet to corroborate or refute the conversion-only incentive structure.
dossiers.bull_case: Full-float, 1-for-1 spinoff (100% of shares distributed day one) with an explicit, if unquantified, index-mismatch selling risk baked into the filing -- the mechanical dump the Nemesis thesis wants. Equity award conversion preserves intrinsic value symmetrically for both Middleby- and SpinCo-side employees, a clean (if not conviction-signaling) alignment structure. Emerging Growth Company status and a brand-new, thinly-followed food-processing-equipment niche (Alkar) suggest real neglect once regular-way trading starts and the dump clears.
dossiers.forced_seller_map: 1-for-1 distribution ratio, 45,214,588 shares = 100% of SpinCo's outstanding shares distributed on day one (no lock-up-limited float). Explicit index-fund risk factor: 'Middleby stockholders who have an investment strategy of tracking an index fund may sell the shares of SpinCo common stock that they receive in the distribution if SpinCo is not listed on the same index.' No specific index name (S&P/Russell) disclosed, and no expected-float percentage quantified -- weaker specificity than some peers in this batch (e.g. MBGL quantifies 29% index-fund ownership), but the mechanism is present and explicit. MFP is a small food-processing-equipment carve-out (Alkar brand) from a mid-cap parent -- plausible small/mid-cap index or style mismatch, unconfirmed.
dossiers.key_risk: Actual pre-spin dividend/debt draw already diverged from the company's own modeled pro forma before the ink was dry, and real standalone dis-synergy costs are explicitly excluded from the earnings picture presented to incoming holders -- the true standalone P&L is unknown until the first post-spin 10-Q.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: N/A -- pre-distribution as of this read. Regular-way MFP trading does not begin until 2026-07-07; only when-issued trading under MFPVV is underway. Zero Form 4s exist (confirmed via EDGAR submissions); only Form 3 initial-ownership statements have been filed for newly appointed officers/directors. Sweep deferred to a post-2026-07-07 run.
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: Company explicitly admits the pro forma excludes real standalone dis-synergy costs: 'such costs are not reflected in the unaudited pro forma condensed combined statement of earnings as these dis-synergies are not considered autonomous entity adjustments.' Same exclusion applies to TSA-related net costs. The $1.0B credit facility terms are stated as 'preliminary and subject to change' -- and the actual 6/29 8-K distribution ($233M, drawn from the full $1.0B facility plus cash on hand) already diverges from the modeled pro forma ($229M dividend funded by $252M of new debt), confirming the estimate was not reliable even in the near term. Share count/dilution effects are also explicitly disclaimed as unestimatable pre-spin.

dossiers.bear_case: $4.3B of new debt funds a $4.1B dividend to FedEx, leaving FDXF with negative pro forma equity of $(562)M day one and a binding leverage covenant (3.75x stepping to 3.50x). The filing explicitly discloses FDXF 'may assume certain liabilities from FedEx ... including some liabilities unrelated to our core business,' specifically flagging pension/healthcare/life-insurance obligations tied to employees 'unrelated to our core business' -- precisely the dumped-liability pattern this strategy screens for, with no dollar figure disclosed to bound the exposure. Tax-matters-agreement indemnification runs one-directionally against FDXF for two years. Zero insiders have bought a single share in the open market one month post-spin, and the entire NEO+director group holds just 0.26% of the company. A second forced-seller wave (FedEx's mandatory 24-month exit of its 19.9% retained stake) still lies ahead.
dossiers.bull_case: FedEx Freight is a large ($8B+ revenue-scale), well-established LTL trucking franchise, not an obscure orphan -- if it retains scale-appropriate index eligibility once the initial and 24-month FedEx-retained-stake dumps clear, a business this size can re-rate on standalone fundamentals once freed from FedEx's slower-growth logistics conglomerate structure.
dossiers.forced_seller_map: Only 80.1% of shares distributed to FedEx holders on day one; FedEx retained 19.9% (29.6M shares) and is contractually required to dispose of the retained stake within 24 months of the spin (via debt-for-equity exchanges or further stockholder distributions) to preserve tax-free treatment -- meaning a SECOND, later wave of forced supply is coming on top of the initial distribution dump. Filing carries an explicit index-fund/style-mismatch risk factor for the initial distribution, but discloses no specific index name or expected-float percentage.
dossiers.key_risk: Explicitly-disclosed assumption of pension/healthcare liabilities 'unrelated to our core business,' undisclosed in dollar terms, stacked on $4.3B of new debt and negative starting equity -- the clearest garbage-barge tell in this batch.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: Form 4 sweep since 2026-06-01 distribution: no open-market purchases, no sales. All 47 post-spin Form 4 transactions across the full officer/director slate are transaction code A (grant/award) at $0 -- matching disclosed Spin-Off RSU/PSU/LTIP grants exactly. Zero conviction-signaling purchases one month post-spin.
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: Historical carve-out costs rely partly on 'a reasonable method of allocation' for shared corporate services -- a judgment-driven input that is the classic vector for optimistic standalone-cost assumptions, unverifiable until real post-spin quarters post. Pro forma diluted EPS explicitly excludes potential dilution from FedEx employees' unvested equity awards ('cannot be fully estimated at this time'). Pension/OPEB participation was accounted for as a multi-employer plan historically, so the historical income statement understates the pension cost burden FDXF assumes directly post-spin. No total dis-synergy dollar estimate is disclosed despite dis-synergies being explicitly flagged as 'expected to be significant.'

dossiers.bear_case: $3.0B of new debt (Term A $1B + Term B $1B + Notes $1B) funded a $2.25B special cash payment to Comcast; underlying business is a structurally declining linear-TV/MVPD franchise (Q1 2026 revenue -1.1%, net income -22%, adjusted EBITDA -7% YoY), which the pro forma's steady-state framing does not capture. Dual-class structure with Roberts-family veto rights makes S&P 500 (re-)inclusion structurally unlikely -- there is no future index-buying catalyst to look forward to, unlike a normal single-class orphan. At 125 trading days post-spin (past the 90-day window), the mechanical dump this strategy is built to buy has very likely already cleared with no re-rating to show for it. No CEO/CFO open-market buying despite large paper grants.
dossiers.bull_case: Large, real forced-seller mechanism (S&P-500-excluded dual-class structure spun from a heavily passively-held parent) plus genuine outside-director cash buying ($6.19M from four independent directors including the new board chair) two months post-spin. First-ever dividend initiated and a $1B buyback authorized despite the leverage, signaling some management confidence in free cash flow generation from the declining-but-cash-generative cable/digital portfolio (GolfNow, Fandango, Rotten Tomatoes, SportsEngine growth assets alongside the legacy linear networks).
dossiers.forced_seller_map: 1-for-25 distribution ratio, ~144.5M shares issued day one, explicit index-fund forced-selling risk factor repeated in both the information statement and the 10-K. Unusually strong specificity here versus other names in this batch: VSNT retains a dual-class structure (Class A / Class B, Roberts family holding 33.33% non-dilutable combined voting power via Class B), and S&P has excluded multi-class share structures from S&P 500 eligibility since 2017 -- meaning the 'may not be included in the S&P 500' risk-factor language is closer to a structural certainty than a hedge. Comcast is a heavily passively-held S&P 500 constituent (Vanguard + BlackRock combined >20% of VSNT post-spin, both purely passive per 13G filings), so the forced-seller mechanism is real and large, but -- at 125 trading days post-spin, past the 90-day window -- whatever dump was coming has very likely already cleared without the stock meaningfully re-rating, and the dual-class exclusion means there is no future index-inclusion catalyst to look forward to.
dossiers.key_risk: The window has already closed (125 trading days, past the 90-day cutoff) with no re-rating and a structurally declining core business behind a dual-class share structure that forecloses future index-inclusion upside -- the thesis this strategy is built around does not apply cleanly here anymore.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: Form 4 sweep since 2026-01-02: 4 insiders made 7 open-market purchases totaling $6,189,514 (Novak $5.27M, Hassell $360.7K, Conway $48.8K, Potter $510K, all in March 2026 at $36-38/share) -- a genuine, board-level vote of confidence with outside cash. But zero open-market purchases by CEO Lazarus or CFO Kini, whose $12.5M/$7.5M 'Founders Grants' are 100% award-conversion paper, not cash purchases. Officer buying is the signal that 'can't be engineered' per this strategy's own weighting; its absence here is a real gap even with the director-level buying as a partial offset. No sales found.
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: Unusually candid: management discloses standalone dis-synergies ($145M 9mo / $273M FY24) as a separate 'Management Adjustments' section outside the formal pro forma, cutting pro forma FY24 EPS ~19% ($7.23 to $5.83) -- a rare instance in this batch of a company volunteering the haircut rather than burying it. Formal pro forma also layers in $134-212M of new commercial/advertising SG&A and $10-45M of one-time systems costs. Actual Q1 2026 net debt ($2,952M) matched the pro forma estimate ($2,955M) almost exactly -- the capital-structure math held up, an honesty tell in that specific line. But revenue/profitability are independently declining (Q1 2026 revenue -1.1%, net income -22%, Adjusted EBITDA -7% YoY) on a structurally shrinking linear-TV/MVPD base -- a real business-quality concern the pro forma's steady-state framing does not surface.

dossiers.bear_case: FPI status means the single most useful signal in this entire framework -- open-market insider buying -- is permanently dark for this name; incentive_alignment can never be verified by behavior, only inferred from paper (weak evidence by this strategy's own hierarchy). Two unremediated material weaknesses in internal controls (journal-entry review/segregation of duties, IT general controls) as of the most recent 10-Q add real near-term financial-reporting risk. Q1 2026 net income down ~20% YoY. $625M debt-funded payment to Hexagon (larger than modeled) transferred roughly $569M of equity value out of the company at spin. No specific index membership confirmed either way.
dossiers.bull_case: Currently in_window (volume normalized, price stabilized, 24 trading days post-spin) -- the mechanical trigger this strategy is built to catch. Liability allocation is business-based and clean: no pension/OPEB liability (DC-only plans), no material litigation or environmental exposure, and only modest historical reliance on shared Hexagon overhead ($11.7M against ~$1.5B revenue) -- the cleanest balance-sheet separation of the batch outside of the debt-funded dividend itself. Retention bonuses with clawbacks give management a real (if modest) reason to stay through year one.
dossiers.forced_seller_map: 1-for-10 distribution ratio (Class A and Class B), ~257.4M Class B + 11.0M Class A shares outstanding. Dual-listing via Swedish Depository Receipts (Nasdaq Stockholm 'OCTV SDB') alongside the Nasdaq US listing adds a second forced-seller channel: index/mandate holders on both sides may be structurally mismatched. Dual-class structure (Class A 10 votes/share, 100% held by MSAB) concentrates 42.9% of voting power with MSAB and -- like VSNT's dual-class setup -- likely impedes major US index eligibility, though no specific index name is confirmed in the filing (weaker specificity than MBGL's quantified 29% index-fund ownership figure). Generic index-fund forced-selling risk factor is present. MSAB's own stake is locked up contractually until May 2027, so no near-term overhang from the largest holder -- the forced selling, if it is occurring, is coming from smaller ex-Hexagon holders whose mandate doesn't fit an FPI orphan, not from MSAB.
dossiers.key_risk: Incentive alignment cannot be verified by any behavioral channel (FPI/Section 16 dark) at the exact moment the mechanical entry trigger has fired -- this name is buyable on the window logic alone, with the qualitative Greenblatt read resting entirely on unverifiable paper incentives and two unremediated control weaknesses.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: FPI -- Section 16 exempt, channel unavailable. Confirmed via filing language ('we will be a foreign private issuer... exempted from... Section 16') and EDGAR header data (insiderTransactionForOwnerExists: 0). Form 4 sweep correctly returns empty and is NOT scored as a negative signal per the FPI caveat. The one Schedule 13D on file (MSAB, filed 2026-06-01) is the mandatory disclosure of MSAB's passive pro-rata receipt of shares in the distribution, not an open-market accumulation -- no accumulation signal available from any channel. Incentive read rests on the Form 10 alone: standard award conversion (neutral) plus one-time cash retention bonuses with a one-year clawback for CEO ($950K), CFO ($800K), CLO ($800K), COO ($300K) -- a real but modest retention-alignment signal, not an ownership one.
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: Classic asymmetric framing: pre-spin restructuring/real-estate savings already actioned by Hexagon are booked as permanent synergies (+$34.7M personnel, +$4.5M non-personnel), while stand-up dis-synergies ($11.2M) are explicitly flagged as one-time/transitory -- the same pattern seen across this batch (HONA, MFP) where transitory costs are assumed away while permanent savings are banked. Actual debt draw at closing ($625M) exceeded the pro forma modeled estimate ($598.2M) by ~$27M, understating leverage at the registration stage -- also consistent with the pattern seen in several peers this cycle (MFP, HONA) where actual financing exceeded modeled figures. Q1 2026 net income declined ~20% YoY ($47.4M vs $59.5M) against an MD&A revenue narrative framed as flat-to-growing -- a real profitability concern not surfaced by the revenue-focused framing.

dossiers.bear_case: Structurally, this is not the trade this strategy is built to make: there is no orphaned spinco, no forced-seller dump into an unwilling-holder vacuum, and no liability-dumping mechanism to screen for, since Sunbelt Rentals IS Ashtead, just redomiciled. The 88-day-old window_state is still 'pre_window' with volume RISING (2.19x) rather than normalizing -- i.e., the mechanical trigger this strategy watches for has not fired and may never fire in the expected direction, because the elevated volume looks driven by earnings/M&A news and index-inclusion anticipation, not by a one-time forced dump working through the market. Management PSU conditions were waived to a favorable historical-average outcome at the transaction's expense -- a minor governance red flag. No insider has bought a share.
dossiers.bull_case: Large ($35B+ market cap), profitable ($1.325B net income FY2026), well-run equipment-rental business with a genuine, filed catalyst: potential future US index inclusion following redomiciliation to Delaware/NYSE-primary listing, which the company itself flags as both an upside driver and a current source of price volatility. Clean balance sheet by this strategy's usual standards -- no debt-funded dividend, no dumped pension/environmental/litigation liability, since the whole company (not a subset) simply changed holding structure. Real M&A (Reliant Asset Management bolt-on announced 2026-06-23) and first annual meeting (Sept 1, 2026) are concrete near-term catalysts.
dossiers.forced_seller_map: This is NOT a classic divisional carve-out -- it is a UK scheme of arrangement redomiciliation: 100% of Ashtead moved into a new Delaware holding company (Sunbelt Rentals Holdings), no divested business unit. The dumped-liability/forced-seller framework built for spinoffs applies only partially. What IS real: Ashtead was a FTSE 100 constituent; post-redomiciliation to Delaware/NYSE-primary listing, UK index trackers plausibly must exit while the company is now newly structurally eligible for US indices (S&P/Russell) it wasn't eligible for before -- the filing itself flags 'potential inclusion in U.S. equity indices' as an anticipated benefit and 'delay in our inclusion in North American indices' as a live stock-price risk factor in the 10-K. This is the inverse of the usual Nemesis setup (anticipated future index BUYING, not post-dump discovery) and is not corroborated by any specific index-fund ownership percentage in the filing. No activist 13D found (only passive 13G: Dodge & Cox 12.8%, Vanguard 7.61%, BlackRock 5.4%).
dossiers.key_risk: The core structural premise of this strategy -- a forced-seller dump into a discarded orphan -- may not apply to a whole-company redomiciliation at all; treat any window-state signal here with extra skepticism versus a true divisional carve-out.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: Form 4 sweep since 2026-03-02: no open-market purchases, no sales. All 43 transactions are RSU/PSU grant conversions (code A, $0) or tax-withholding sales (code F) tied to routine vesting -- no discretionary insider buying or selling of any kind. Rules out both an insider-conviction explanation and an insider-panic explanation for the rising volume.
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: No pro forma financial statements exist in this filing -- because the whole company moved (not a carve-out), historical financials are identical pre/post transaction. There is no adjustment schedule to interrogate for hidden allocations, and correspondingly no standalone-cost or dis-synergy estimate exists to be optimistic about. The one real economic transfer found: PSU performance conditions on outstanding management awards were WAIVED and deemed met at 85.5% of maximum (the historical 5-year average) rather than actually measured going forward -- a value transfer to management at the transaction's expense, not a garbage-barge liability dump but a governance-quality flag worth weighing against incentive alignment.

dossiers.bear_case: A 217x volume spike is unexplained by any SEC filing -- the record goes dark after a routine 2026-06-22 sublease 8-K, and no insider buying corroborates conviction. The most plausible lead (the unresolved rosnilimab strategic-alternatives process) is exactly the kind of pending binary event that turns this into a coin-flip on deal terms rather than a forced-seller re-rating play -- the window/dump-supply model this strategy is built around may simply not apply if the current move is catalyst-driven. Management's own 'two-year runway' framing does not match its formal 12-month going-concern language, a real credibility gap on the one number prospective holders most need to trust.
dossiers.bull_case: Launched cash-rich (~$180M, no debt-funded dividend or dumped liability of any kind -- the cleanest balance sheet in this cohort), with genuinely price-linked PSU vesting hurdles for management (a rarity among the peers reviewed, most of which use mere-time or mere-distribution triggers). Retains upside from a Vanda Collaboration milestone stream (up to $35M). A live, disclosed strategic-alternatives process for its lead asset rosnilimab (partnership/out-license/asset-financing) could deliver a real near-term catalyst if it resolves favorably.
dossiers.forced_seller_map: 1-for-1 distribution ratio, sub-$250M public float confirmed by the filing's own smaller-reporting-company disclosure -- a genuinely small, thin-float spinout from a mid-cap biotech parent. Explicit 'may not fit investor mandate' risk-factor language present, but no specific index-fund ownership percentage disclosed. The one large insider transaction in the tape ($65M EcoR1 sale) was a pre-arranged Secondary Shares sale into a Private Placement disclosed in the Form 10, not organic index-fund dumping -- so the textbook forced-seller mechanic is present in form (small float, mandate mismatch) but not clearly evidenced in the actual trading tape reviewed.
dossiers.key_risk: Unexplained, extreme volume spike coincides with an unresolved strategic-alternatives process on the lead asset -- this name may be trading on binary deal-rumor risk, not on the forced-seller dump this strategy is designed to buy; requires an external news check before any entry decision, ghost or live.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: Form 4 sweep since 2026-04-06: no open-market purchases; one insider (EcoR1 Capital, 10%+ holder) sold $64,999,990 on 2026-04-20 -- but this was the pre-negotiated Secondary Shares component of a disclosed Private Placement, not opportunistic dumping (EcoR1 retained a 9.1% stake per its 13G). Six directors show only routine option/RSU exercises (code M, ~$0). No insider has put a dollar of open-market cash behind the stock since the spin, despite PSU awards carrying genuine spinco stock-price hurdles ($75/$100, the most price-linked vesting structure in this batch).
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: The clearest pro-forma-honesty gap in this batch: management's own marketing language claims a 'two-year cash runway' (repeated in the cover letter and Q1 2026 earnings release), but the formal, legally-operative going-concern/liquidity disclosure in both the information statement and the 10-Q commits only to 'at least the next 12 months.' FY2025 burn was $142.9M and Q1 2026 alone showed a $50.5M net loss (burn did not decelerate post-separation) -- against ~$180M of launch cash this implies roughly 14-15 months of runway, not 24. This is a quotable, material gap between the story told to prospective holders and the company's own formal risk disclosure.

dossiers.bear_case: Only 3 trading days old -- essentially no data yet on management's actual behavior, and the paper incentive story (standard RSU/PSU conversion, hired CEO with no founder stake) offers no strong signal either way. $1.9B debt-funded payment to S&P Global funded by $2B of new senior notes at 5.05-6.05% is a real leverage step-up from an effectively debt-free starting point, and TSA-masked stranded costs will hit after 18 months in ways the pro forma does not model.
dossiers.bull_case: The cleanest, most explicitly-quantified forced-seller setup in this cohort (29% index-fund ownership, explicit S&P 500 exclusion expectation) on a substantial, real business ($1.75B revenue, CARFAX brand) -- large enough to matter, small/mismatched enough to be structurally excluded from its parent's index. No pension/OPEB, no material litigation or environmental liability, moderate (not extreme) leverage relative to peers in this batch.
dossiers.forced_seller_map: 1-for-1 distribution ratio, ~295M shares, directors/execs own <0.05% so essentially the entire float is immediately tradable. The strongest-quantified forced-seller mechanic in this entire batch: the filing states 'we believe approximately 29% of S&P Global's outstanding common stock is held by index funds' and explicitly expects it will NOT be included in the S&P 500 or other indices S&P Global belongs to, meaning roughly 85M of ~295M shares are held by mechanical trackers required to sell with no offsetting natural demand -- textbook Nemesis setup, and notably ironic given the parent is itself the index provider.
dossiers.key_risk: Far too early for any behavioral confirmation (insider buying, price stabilization) -- the mechanical forced-seller setup is the best-quantified in this batch, but the window is still pre_window and this dossier will need a real update once data accumulates.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: N/A -- too early to read. Zero Form 4 transactions exist as of this sweep (3 trading days post-distribution); only 10 Form 3 initial-ownership statements have been filed. Disclosed NEO/director ownership is already negligible (<0.05% aggregate, all hired-operator executives with no founder stake) -- not a bearish signal this early, but also no positive corroboration yet. Revisit in 1-2 weeks once the Form 4 window has had time to populate.
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: Company explicitly discloses that ongoing standalone corporate costs (SOX compliance, new executive comp, IR/reporting) are 'not quantifiable at this time' and excluded from the pro forma income statement. TSA-related incremental costs are also small/deferred now ($1-3M) but the TSA only runs 18 months, after which Mobility must replace shared S&P Global IT/finance/HR infrastructure at its own cost -- a stranded-cost cliff not modeled. Total liabilities more than double ($1,459M to $3,235M pro forma) while equity shrinks $1,656M, funding a $1.9B cash payment to S&P Global, framed as 'consideration for asset transfer' rather than a value transfer.

dossiers.bear_case: New $150M CD&R-held Series A preferred stock carries a 7% cash/PIK dividend escalating to 10% on a Triggering Event, and can BLOCK ALL COMMON DIVIDENDS while a Triggering Event is occurring -- a real, structurally senior claim on the business that functions similarly to a dumped liability even though it's equity-labeled. The Tax Matters Agreement creates a poison-pill-like overhang: any acquisition of ADI shares that breaks tax-free treatment makes ADI responsible for the resulting tax liability, discouraging the very M&A/re-rating catalyst an orphan spinoff would otherwise hope for. $900M dividend plus $67M cash payment to Resideo, funded by $1B of new debt at a blended rate materially above the historical allocated cost. No insider or ownership data exists yet to offset any of this.
dossiers.bull_case: Legacy Honeywell environmental indemnification (from Resideo's own 2018 spin) was fully resolved and paid by Resideo pre-spin via a $1.59B settlement -- ADI inherits none of that overhang, a genuine positive versus a name that might otherwise carry legacy-parent liability. Governance is reasonably clean (double-trigger change-in-control vesting, no single-trigger acceleration at spin). Pro forma candor on near-term unprofitability is a positive honesty signal relative to peers.
dossiers.forced_seller_map: 1-for-2 distribution ratio, ~75.4M shares. CD&R Channel Holdings II retains 19.69% (a real, disclosed sponsor overhang, though CD&R also holds the new preferred so its incentives are aligned with the capital structure, not necessarily with common holders). BlackRock 13.02%, Dimensional 5.21%, Vanguard 5.00% -- ~23.2% combined passive/quant ownership received mechanically regardless of index fit. Circumstantial evidence (Resideo's PSU awards reference the S&P SmallCap 600 as a TSR benchmark) suggests Resideo sits in that index; ADI's own prospective index membership is not disclosed either way.
dossiers.key_risk: The new senior preferred stock's escalating-rate, dividend-blocking terms are a structurally embedded claim ahead of common equity that functions like a dumped liability, compounded by a tax-driven poison-pill provision that discourages the M&A catalyst this strategy's orphans often need to re-rate.
dossiers.post_spin_insider_activity: N/A -- pre-distribution (Distribution Date August 3, 2026, still about a month out). No Form 4 filer relationship exists yet under this CIK. Sweep deferred to a post-distribution run.
dossiers.pro_forma_notes: Unusually candid on near-term unprofitability: pro forma Q1 2026 net loss available to common stockholders of $(6)M, EPS $(0.08) -- rare for a spinoff information statement to show a per-share loss rather than a dressed-up profit. But the $51M FY2025 / $8M Q1-2026 dis-synergy estimates are managements' own soft estimates likely to be revised upward once TSA services are actually replaced, and the pro forma interest expense (~$68-71M on the new $1B debt at ~6.78%) is materially higher than the $44M historically allocated -- a real, disclosed leverage step-up easy for a reader to skip past.
